saida_bruta:
There was nothing gigantic about the man just beginning to stir on the sleeping-porch of a Dutch Colonial house in Zenith’s Floral Heights district.

His name was George F. Babbitt. Now forty-six, in April 1920, he produced nothing in particular—neither butter nor shoes nor poetry—yet he excelled at the trade of selling houses for more than buyers could truly afford.

His large head was pink, his brown hair thin and dry. In sleep his face looked babyish, for all the wrinkles and the red marks left by spectacles on the sides of his nose. He was not fat, yet thoroughly well nourished; his cheeks were padded, and the smooth hand lying limp on the khaki blanket was slightly puffy. He appeared prosperous, thoroughly married, and wholly unromantic; equally unromantic was the sleeping-porch itself, overlooking one large elm, two tidy lawns, a concrete drive, and a corrugated-iron garage. Yet Babbitt was once more dreaming of the fairy child, a vision more romantic than scarlet pagodas beside a silver sea.

His large head was pink, his brown hair thin and dry. Asleep, his face seemed almost boyish despite the wrinkles and the red spectacle marks along his nose. Yet once again Babbitt dreamed of the fairy child, a vision far more romantic than scarlet pagodas beside a silver sea.
